one_line_takeaway: A theory paper -- with explicitly fabricated/illustrative rather than measured experimental tables -- arguing via a Markov-kernel/mutual-information analysis that diffusion-based image editing structurally erodes robust invisible watermarks (even under benign, non-adversarial edits) because forward noising destroys channel capacity and reverse denoising contracts off-manifold watermark residuals toward the natural-image manifold.
key_finding: The paper's real (theoretical) claim is that watermark recoverability decays with diffusion edit strength through two compounding mechanisms it derives: (1) forward noising shrinks the effective channel SNR and mutual information between payload and noised image as alpha_bar_t* -> 0 (Theorem 6.1), driving Fano's bound toward near-certain message error (Corollary 6.1); and (2) even independent of noising strength, the reverse denoising process acts as a contraction toward the natural-image manifold that exponentially suppresses off-manifold watermark residuals over n denoising steps (rho^n * ||delta||, Proposition 6.1). Together these are offered as a mechanistic/information-theoretic explanation for why 'robust to conventional post-processing' watermarks are not robust to diffusion-based semantic editing, including localized edits that only modify part of an image (because diffusion coupling via attention/latent denoising is global, not local). The illustrative tables are consistent-by-construction with this theory (not independent evidence for it), since the authors state they hand-picked hypothetical numbers to match the qualitative direction of the theory and cited literature.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (Sec 1.1, formalized in Sec 3 and Sec 6) is: (1) a formulation of diffusion-based image editing as a Markov kernel over watermarked images, composed of controlled noising + conditional denoising + optional architectural operations (attention reweighting, region constraints, latent optimization); (2) a theoretical analysis of watermark degradation under this kernel, deriving Theorem 6.1 (an upper bound on mutual information I(M;X_t*) between the watermark payload M and the noised sample, via a Gaussian-channel argument) and Corollary 6.1 (a Fano-inequality lower bound on message error probability), plus Proposition 6.1 (an exponential/contraction-based bound showing denoising trajectories suppress off-manifold watermark residuals under an assumed local-contraction property, Assumption 6.1); (3) a proposed evaluation protocol, 'DEW-ST' (Diffusion Editing Watermark Stress Test, Algorithm 1), spanning instruction-based, drag-based, and training-free composition editors against representative post-hoc watermarking systems; and (4) a conceptual diffusion-augmented training objective (Algorithm 2) and design guidelines for 'diffusion-resilient' watermarking. Sections 2.1-2.5 (diffusion editing methods, neural watermarking systems, generator-integrated watermarks, watermark-removal/regeneration literature, concept erasure) are explicitly Related Work citing prior authors (Ho et al. 2020, Brooks et al. 2023, Tancik et al. 2020, Bui et al. 2025, Lu et al. 2025c/VINE, Wen et al. 2023/Tree-Ring, Fernandez et al. 2023/Stable Signature, Zhao et al. 2024b, Ni et al. 2025, etc.) and are not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: The paper defines three explicit evaluation regimes (Sec 3.6), with the first as its primary focus: (1) Benign editing (unintentional degradation) -- a user edits an image for aesthetic/semantic reasons with no intent to strip provenance signals; this is the paper's stated primary focus. (2) Opportunistic editing -- a user who suspects editing weakens watermarks chooses an editor/instruction that happens to degrade recovery, treating the editor as a black box with no decoder access. (3) Adaptive adversarial editing (decoder-in-the-loop) -- an attacker explicitly optimizes the diffusion sampling trajectory using decoder gradients to minimize detectability; the paper states this regime is 'important for security analysis' but is discussed only to contextualize worst-case risk and is not the paper's empirical/theoretical focus (it cites Ni et al. 2025 for that adversarial setting).
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations (Sec 8.5, 5 points): (1) empirical results are illustrative -- the full benchmark protocol (DEW-ST) was not actually executed in this manuscript; (2) diffusion editors and watermarking implementations evolve rapidly, so any fixed benchmark risks obsolescence; (3) the theoretical analysis relies on simplified assumptions -- an additive watermark signal model and an idealized local-contraction/manifold assumption (Assumption 6.1) that is stated, not empirically verified; (4) adaptive adversarial attacks that explicitly optimize against the watermark decoder (cited: Ni et al. 2025, Guo et al. 2026) can be stronger than the unintentional-editing regime the paper emphasizes; (5) generator-integrated defenses (e.g., diffusion-native fingerprints) may be incompatible with real-world provenance requirements that need to watermark arbitrary pre-existing images post hoc.

datasets_used: COCO val (proposed at 5k images) -- named in the DEW-ST protocol (Table 3) as the intended evaluation corpus; not run
ImageNet val (proposed at 5k images) -- named in the DEW-ST protocol; not run
DIV2K (proposed at 800 images) -- named in the DEW-ST protocol; not run
quantitative_results: [ILLUSTRATIVE, self-admitted hypothetical, NOT measured -- Table 4] StegaStamp bit accuracy: 99.4% (watermarked-only baseline) -> 71.5% (InstructPix2Pix, t*=0.4) -> 53.2% (InstructPix2Pix, t*=0.8), vs. random-guess floor of ~50%
[ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT measured -- Table 4] TrustMark bit accuracy: 99.7% baseline -> 76.1% (IP2P t*=0.4) -> 55.0% (IP2P t*=0.8)

[ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT measured -- Table 4] VINE bit accuracy: 99.8% baseline -> 85.4% (IP2P t*=0.4) -> 60.7% (IP2P t*=0.8); the paper frames VINE as the most robust of the three across illustrative editing conditions, attributed to its diffusion-aware training

[ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT measured -- Table 4] conventional post-processing retains high bit accuracy for comparison: JPEG q50 gives 96.1%/98.2%/98.9% for StegaStamp/TrustMark/VINE respectively, vs. sharp drops under diffusion editing

[ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT measured -- Table 11] Detector AUC for generator-integrated provenance methods under diffusion editing: Tree-Ring 0.99 (post-processing) -> 0.92 (same-model edit) -> 0.61 (cross-model edit); Stable Signature 0.98 -> 0.89 -> 0.58; SynthID-Image 0.99 -> 0.90 -> 0.65; post-hoc TrustMark 0.97 -> 0.74 -> 0.72

[ILLUSTRATIVE, NOT measured -- Table 12] hypothetical defense ablation: diffusion-edit augmentation during watermark training raises mild-edit bit accuracy from 74.0% (standard noise-layer training) to 88.0% (with diffusion augmentation + multi-scale embedding), but strong-edit bit accuracy only rises from 54.5% to 60.4%, i.e. augmentation does not eliminate failure under strong edits
baselines_compared: StegaStamp (Tancik et al., 2020) -- pixel-domain robust watermark, proposed as one of three watermarking systems evaluated in the (unexecuted) protocol
TrustMark (Bui et al., 2025) -- resolution-scaling post-hoc watermark, proposed baseline
VINE (Lu et al., 2025c) -- diffusion-aware trained watermark, proposed baseline

Editors named in the protocol as the transformation family under study (not baselines in the classic sense): InstructPix2Pix, an UltraEdit-trained instruction editor, DragDiffusion, InstantDrag, DragFlow, TF-ICON, SHINE
Generator-integrated provenance methods discussed comparatively (Table 11, also illustrative): Tree-Ring, Stable Signature, SynthID-Image
